author: @nerminhamidovic638
text:
Furthering my previous comments regarding the stator. Please also consider the maximum power transfer theorem  where the maximum power that can be delivered to the load will be at the point where the load matches the equivalent thavenin resistance of the supply which will be function of the output voltage and current. in a low wind situation you will have a problem where anything around 1V generated through the coils will not even go through the diodes whereas if it was series wired and unrectified it would already generate high enough voltages to rectify and extract useful energy
The MPPTs that use a incremental conductance algorithm will on the fly vary the output load to ensure that the maximum power is being extracted from the (solar or wind). So you need to also consider the loads and maximum power transfer theorem. I.e static loads will collaps your input quickly. 
So it may be better to have the highest voltage possible and matched loads using MPPT that won't collapse the turbine. This will help with wind intermittency due to retained inertia and required cut in speeds.
I hope this helps

author: @lloydtaylor4635

replies.author: @lloydtaylor4635
replies.text: Is there a time limit to how long a battery will “hold”a charge? If a battery array could hold a charge for months, it might be easier to keep it “topped up” as the weather cooperates (wind/sun)!! Ideally we could charge a large battery during summertime and store it for cold winter nights!!

author: @GusF123
text: Once you get those magnets and coils under it (assuming that's still the placement), it would be interesting to mirror them on the top. I'm sure it would be pushing the limits of amount of energy that can be extracted, but again, we're talking about the low end of things. While it should be impossible to exceed the total theoretical limit of energy conversion, it sure would be interesting to see how close to 99.9% you could get!

author: @NorthernKitty
text: That was still spinning after two-and-a-half minutes!  Impressively balanced, considering you bent those fins by hand... really goes to show how easy it is for anyone to fabricate a decent wind turbine with junk parts and a minimum of tools. 😊  The welding of the frame seemed to be the only moderately challenging task (only because it requires equipment/skill/experience), but honestly a person could probably just bolt together a frame like that.  Both amazing results and inspiring!!

author: @Aerotech007
text: Looks great Rob & spins better than my MK1, which you have inspired me to build out of scrap i had lying around. The hardest thing for me is limiting myself not to spend any money on it, everything has to be done for free, but this is the most rewarding point about this project, stretching my mind to make something work out of what i have, not what i could buy. My frame is from 2 old kitchen stools, the fan blades from excess black plastic toilet pipe, Perspex from a broken computer screen, some Meccano i had from childhood, a pulley and magnets my son had, a sink plug drain fashioned into a bearing, some copper pipe & elbows from my plumbing box, some coils from two scrap microwaves which were fly tipped near me. I know some of the comments you have received have been a bit thoughtless relating to this project, but please keep in mind that you are inspiring a lot of people who really value what you do, who really look forward to every video you make & who really appreciate that you share your incredible knowledge for all because you want too, not because you have too.

replies.author: @edwyncorteen1527
replies.text: This sort of rotor does not need this unlike "normal" wind mills, they are self controlling, as the wind speed increases the drag on the opposite side of the rotor that generates rotational force also increases slowing it down, additionally they do not care which direction the wind comes from, ideal in a built environment.

author: @popuptoaster
text: A sudden thought occurs to me, if you find you have to much resistance at low speed once you are generating power from it  you could move the magnets and coils further inwards for better mechanical advantage, it might take some experimentation to find the right "gear ratio" for optimum low speed wind performance, of course if it spins TO fast even with them coils at the outer edge you might need to run t through a chain and sprocket to speed up the generator. I am interested to see what happens, I suspect these will quieter in the wind than a bladed turbine and if it can survive higher winds might be worth trying on my boat.
